params.text:
CQC standards and your duty of care

The Care Quality Commission regulates care services against fundamental standards. Regulation 13: Safeguarding service users from abuse and improper treatment requires providers to protect people from abuse, avoidable harm, degrading treatment and the unnecessary or unlawful use of restraint. Every member of staff must know how to prevent, recognise and report abuse. Meeting Regulation 13 is not just a legal obligation for your employer - it depends on each of us doing our part every day.

params.alt: A carer holding the hand of an older person

params.text:
Key points to remember

  • The Care Act 2014 and its six principles frame all adult safeguarding, and Making Safeguarding Personal keeps the person at the centre.
  • Learn the categories of abuse and neglect and stay alert to changes and patterns of signs.
  • Respect mental capacity and consent, but never let them stop you reporting serious risk.
  • If in doubt, recognise, respond, record and report - and never promise to keep abuse secret.
  • Speak up through whistleblowing when care is unsafe, and treat Prevent concerns as safeguarding concerns.

Safeguarding is everyone's business. When you act promptly and honestly, you help the people you support to live in safety and with dignity.

params.question: Which statement best sums up your safeguarding responsibility as a frontline care worker?

answers.text: To recognise possible abuse, respond safely, record the facts and report the concern promptly to the right person.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Recognise, respond, record and report - that is the heart of your everyday safeguarding role.

answers.text: To investigate concerns fully and gather proof before telling anyone.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Investigating is not your role and waiting for proof can leave people at risk.

answers.text: To act only when a manager specifically instructs you to.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Safeguarding is everyone's responsibility. You must act on any genuine concern yourself.

answers.text: To keep concerns confidential if the person asks you to.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. You can never promise to keep abuse secret when someone may be at risk of harm.
